Have students search the internet for products in our world that use the word kinetic linked to motion.

Why do Objects at a Height have Potential Energy? (Can students answer this?) Objects at a height are naturally pulled to the ground by gravity and therefore they have potential energy to do work as they fall. If a rock is dropped from a ladder onto a mango by mistake, there may be a dent in the mango. The rock in falling does work on the mango when it impacts it.

Questions about the swinging pendulum: 1) At which numbered point(s) do you think the potential energy of the swinging weight is highest? (1 & 5) 2) At which numbered point(s) do you think the kinetic energy of the swinging weight is highest? (3) 3) At which numbered point(s) do you think potential energy and kinetic energy could be similar? (2 & 4) 4) After you release the pendulum bob (weight) and it begins to swing downward, does it ever regain totally the potential energy it had in the beginning? If not, why not? (The pendulum weight will swing back and forth until it rests in the middle.
This is because air friction causes the initial amount of energy to be slowly lost so that less is available to convert to kinetic energy.

Plot a graph of the speed of the train (x axis) versus. the distance the box traveled (y axis). Questions: 1. What did you notice about the shape of the graph? (It rises to the right). 2. What does the graph imply about the relationship between speed and distance? (as speed increases the distance the box moves also increases- they have a direct relationship). 3. Multiple choice question:

Possible Answers: a. The box would move the same distance as a train moving at 70 cm/s. b. The box would move a shorter distance. c. The box would move a longer distance. d. The box would move the same distance as a train moving at 30 cm/s. Correct answer: c. The box would move a longer distance. Explanation: Peter's investigation was testing how the distance a box moved would change as the speed of a toy train increased or decreased. The pattern within the data table shows that the higher the rate of the train, the further the box moved. An inference can be made that the higher something's speed is, the more energy it has. Higher amounts of energy will result in a more significant change of motion for the object it is acting on. When the speed decreases, the distance the box moves also decreases. There is a direct relationship between speed and energy. So if be were to increase the speed of the train, the box would move a longer distance. (Usain Bolt, the fastest man alive, he is from Jamaica) What is he doing? (running) What does he need in order to be able to move and run? Riding a bike, Running around the field, Cleaning the chalkboard, Sweeping the classroom

What other activities do you do that require energy? [Write down on the chalk board activities that require energy that the students do.] When you are performing these activities, what are you doing? (Work) Where do you get the energy you need to do work? (From the food we eat) Energy is often defined as the ability to do work.

Recognizing Different Types of Energy In certain parts of world like China they have created energy using water. Look at this picture of the Three Gorges Dam. It took many years to build and produces the most energy of any hydropower dam in the world. Hydro means water; yes, we get power from water! Retrieved from: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Three_Gorges_Dam#/media/File:Three_GorgesDam-China2009.jpg This technology is actually very old. For many years, settlers in countries around the world have decided to build their homes near water so they could access power.

The water in a river above a waterfall can fall downward and turn a wheel. In the picture below you can see water has been directed from above to turn this wheel if the wheel is attached to an axle in the centre, it can create energy to do work like grinding grain or lifting a load. Retrieved from: https://energyeducation.ca/encyclopedia/Waterwheel. At the top of the wheel before the waterfalls, we say the water has potential energy to do work- sometimes referred to as energy of position. The word potential means it has the possibility to do work. Let me use that word in a sentence “My students have the potential to be wonderful parents and productive citizens!” We sometimes hear that word used around batteries for our radio or flashlight. That is because the battery has the potential to do work; play music through a speaker or light a lightbulb! When the water is pulled downward by gravity, it begins to have a new type of energy of motion called kinetic energy. In modern hydropower they use special wheels called turbines to take the energy from large volumes of flowing water.

Retrieved from https://www.micro-hydro-power.com/hydro-turbine-generator/. The turning wheel is connected to something called a generator that creates electricity from the kinetic energy of the moving water. Video Resource for Potential and Kinetic Energy. Let us now watch a video where we can learn more about energy. https://youtu.be/Q0LBegPWzrg?si=mixpy9IyS5hr9e8O (4:13 mins) Kinetic and Potential energy stops at 2:13 mins

What are the two main types of energy mentioned in the video? (Kinetic Energy and Potential Energy). What did you learn about kinetic energy? What did you learn about potential energy? Students share their initial understanding. Other Examples of Potential and Kinetic Energy. There are two types of energy: Potential Energy: stored energy, energy ready to go. Example, stretching a rubber band, a race car at the starting line, a rock that rests at the top of a cliff.

Kinetic Energy: is energy at work example releasing a rubber band, a rock falling from a cliff Teacher will provide students with rubber bands. Stretch your rubber band between your fingers. What kind of energy does the stretched rubber band possess? (Answer: when the elastic band was stretched it gained potential energy. We know this because your hand had to do some work to stretch the elastic band.) Release your rubber band, causing it to shoot across the room. What kind of energy is now present when the rubber band is released? (caution students not to release in the direction of classmates or their own faces) An elastic band when twisted can build up potential energy; enough to spin a propeller on a light-weight wooden plane. The winding of the elastic builds potential energy and the release that spins the propeller is kinetic energy of motion. Retrieved from: https://youtu.be/hPf4d7-2G9Q A sling shot also involves stretching a rubber band to build potential energy and then releasing it with a pebble enclosed to quickly attain kinetic energy of motion. Linking Speed and Energy

Students, I am sure you have heard the word speed before. If I told you that a red car was traveling faster than a blue car, which car likely has the greater speed? (The red car would have the greater speed) Speed is the distance travelled in a period of time. The car that goes the same distance in a shorter time will always be the higher speed. Materials: stack of books, smooth wooden ramp, toy car, watch to measure time, ruler. NOTE: Can be done as a teacher demonstration or by individual class groups. We are going to release a car to roll down a ramp from different positions and measure how far it travels from the bottom of the ramp in the same amount of time (e.g. 10 seconds-measure by stopwatch) If you had to guess students, which car would go the fastest, the one released from point A or point B? (point A). If you had to guess students, which car would go the furthest, the one released from point A or point B? (point A). Which car would have the largest potential energy? (point A car because it is the highest) So let us try this and confirm our prediction. Over 10 seconds

Release from point A – distance traveled= _____ cm Release from point B – distance traveled= _____ cm Teacher notes We know that the car released from point A went a further distance in the same amount of time (10 secs) so it is going faster than the car released from point B. We also know that because point A is higher, the car released from there has more potential energy. As the car goes down the ramp, all the potential energy is converted to kinetic energy of motion. Our final conclusion is: The car released from point A will have the higher speed and the higher potential energy and that car goes the farthest. This means energy and speed are directly related, the higher potential energy the higher the speed. The earth’s gravity we feel every day (referred to as weight) causes objects at a height to have the ability to fall to the ground. This type of contained ability is called “Potential Energy”. As something begins to move it has speed and takes on a new type of energy of motion called Kinetic Energy. Motion can be described in terms of speed, and it requires energy in order to set something in motion. Therefore, speed is related to energy.

What Fuels are Used for Energy in Your Community Students should work in groups to create a one-page graphic organizer showing all the different fuels that are used in their community for transportation, heating, industry etc.

Negative Affects of Burning Fossil Fuels for Energy The teacher should divide the class into two opposing teams to have a debate about the pollution impacts of burning fossil fuels (oil, diesel, natural gas, coal). The question: Can we tolerate the amount of pollution generated by burning fossil fuels given the extreme need in our communities for inexpensive energy? In the discussion, the teacher should challenge students to consider: 1) the levels of pollution that different fuels generate. Are some tolerable and others not? 2) Where is air pollution most prevalent in their communities (i.e. streets, industry, farms etc.). Where is it tolerable and where should it be most avoided?

Heat and Light as Energy Sources: Student response paragraph: In times gone by, fire was an important form of energy that provided heat and light. Research Tsunamis: Students should be asked to look up information on the internet regarding tsunamis. In a one-page response paper, have students explain what a tsunami is using a drawn picture if they wish. They must answer the question “how does a tsunami demonstrate the transfer of energy? (Violent motion in the earth’s crust creates a wave that travels a long distance and is felt in the form of wave surges on the beach and resultant flooding.) inclusive_learning_strategies: Students, I am sure you have heard the word energy before. Has a parent or teacher ever asked you to do something and you have replied “I just don’t have the energy!” What do you mean when you say that? (I am tired, my body is sore, it is too late in the day) A simple definition of energy is the ability to do work.
Besides the energy we can produce (physical work) by eating a balanced diet, getting exercise and caring for our health, there are other types of energy all around us!
Where do you see energy around us in everyday life? (lights, motion, heat, sound, waves)
Energy exists in many forms. Based on the pictures below, are you able to guess the forms of energy?
(The students will be engaged in a guided discussion in order to arrive at the answers.)
1. What form of energy does television use? Answer: [dropdown]
2. What form of energy does light bulb emits? Answer: [dropdown]
3. What form of energy does fire emits? Answer: [dropdown]
4. What form of energy does speakers emits? Answer: [dropdown]
5. What form of energy does the sun emits? Answer: [dropdown]
Forms of Energy We will focus on four forms of energy.

1. Sound Energy Sound is the movement of energy through substances in waves. Sound occurs when a force causes an object to vibrate. These vibrations create sound waves. Sound energy is used in radios, IPOds, and for the generation of music. Sound is used for communication by animals including humans

2. Light Energy Light is a form of energy that allows us to see. The sun is the ultimate source of light energy (called solar energy. Solar energy can be used to cook, keep warm, dry clothes and create electricity.) but there are other forms of light energy such as light given off by a candle or an electric bulb. We can use light energy to see or to power objects such as solar calculators or solar panels that can be used to generate electricity. Plants need light energy to produce their own food.

3. Electrical Energy Electrical energy is the movement of charged particles. Electrical energy comes from other forms of energy that are changed. We commonly use this form of energy to power things in our homes. Examples of electrical energy include objects that run on batteries. Batteries create a flow of particles (electricity) when a circuit is complete. Lightning is an example of a natural form of electrical energy.

4. Thermal or Heat Energy Thermal energy is a form of energy that we often refer to as heat energy. Thermal energy is caused by the increased activity of the molecules (or tiny parts) that make up an object. When these particles vibrate very rapidly, heat is produced. Our greatest source of thermal energy is the Sun. We use thermal energy to heat our water and our homes. Some examples of thermal energy include a campfire, water boiling on a stove, and an oven.
How Does Energy Get Transferred? Teacher demonstrations

Example 1: Motion of waves Fill a large tub with water. Put a small floating boat at one end. At the other end of the tub agitate the water using a flat plastic cover. Ask the students why the boat at the other end of the tub begins to move? (The energy you put into the wave travels across the water and is felt by the boat in its movement.) Reiterate- energy can travel!

Example 2: Sound is a wave Access a small drum (or stretch the latex of a balloon over a large jar top and fasten the sides so that you have a membrane covering the bottle.) Place a small amount of sand on the top of the drum skin. Next to the drum play a sound from a radio or music player (or tuning fork if you have one). Ask the students why the sand on the drum skin begins to jump? (Sound is a form of energy as a wave and as it travels by the drum skin it passes energy to the membrane causing it to vibrate.) Reiterate- energy can travel!

Example 3: Heat energy: Cut a 15 cm piece of metal coat hanger and dip one end in hot wax from a burning candle. Let the wax solidify. Hold the hanger at one end and heat the middle of the metal with a candle flame. Ask students why the wax melted.? (Eventually, the heat energy will be transferred along the metal and cause the wax at the end to get soft and melt. Reiterate- energy can travel! Ask students if they have ever seen a pot or fry pan with a rubber or plastic handle? What is the purpose? (to prevent be transferred heat energy from heating up your hand)

Brief history on fire- An Example of Energy transformation- motion to heat: We are fortunate to have matches and stoves in order for us to cook our delicious meals, but do you know how far back we have been using fire? (cavemen days or 300 000 to 400 000 years ago) Back in the days, people had to be creative in order to survive and making fire was one of them. In order to create fire, ancient humans converted motion (which requires work and hence energy) to heat. Can you make a fire without matches or kerosene? Take a look at the video below, will you be able to make a fire on your own to survive in the wild?
Tools which were used to make fire Retrieved from: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bxjqz2_-Zc4
More Examples of Energy Transformations: Student experiment Materials: batteries (dry cells), connecting wires, switch, light bulb, flashcards (with the terms: electrical, heat, light)

Students will be placed in mixed ability groups and given materials to build a circuit. Students will be asked to use the materials provided to make the bulb light. The students will play with the materials and eventually build a circuit. When is the circuit an open circuit? (The switch is open) Students please leave your circuit open. What form of energy is present in the open circuit? (electrical potential energy) We call it this because the battery has potential to do work as soon as we give it an opportunity. Students, please close your circuit. Let us leave it on for a minute. What happened to the bulb? (It lit up) What form of energy was produced? (Light energy) Place your hand around the bulb. What do you feel? (Heat/ bulb is hot) What form of energy was produced? (Heat energy) What energy was present before we put on our switch/complete the circuit? (electrical potential energy) What happens to this energy? (changed form) What new forms of energy were produced? (heat and light) We have just observed that energy transformation took place. What do you think is energy transformation? The teacher will have the students formulate a definition for energy transformation. (Changing of energy from one form to another.)

We can develop a flowchart to represent the energy transformation that took place. Students can be given the three forms of energy and the arrows on flash cards and can be asked in their groups to create the flowchart.

More Examples of Energy Transformations: Materials: Electric kettle, tea, sugar, milk, cell phone, ice, towel, flashlight, flash cards, markers. The teacher will set up the following workstations around the classroom and/or around the school in order for students to observe, write down the energy transformations/conversions and then create flow charts. Guiding questions can be posed to the students/placed at the workstations to help them identify the energy conversions such as: What form of energy is powering up the object/device? What energy form of energy is being generated when this device/object is being used? If the object is left on/used for a long time, what would happen to it?
Workstation 1: Using an electric kettle to boil water for making tea. Energy transformation observed: electrical energy → heat energy → sound energy
Workstation 2: Using the cellphone to play a video. Energy transformation observed: chemical potential energy → Electrical energy → light energy → sound energy and heat energy
Workstation 3: Use a remote-control car. Energy transformation observed: Chemical Potential energy → electrical energy → kinetic energy → sound energy → heat energy
Workstation 4: Using a fan to cool the room. Energy transformation observed: Electrical energy → kinetic → sound energy and heat energy

Alternatively: A video demonstrating various energy transformations can be played. Students can identify the energy transformations and create the flow charts based on the examples from the video. The video reveals the flow charts for each example.

All images retrieved from: https://youtu.be/PKm4ZVNmjyQ?si=fN0tNMfOEipKkjH (4:10 mins) The video has 8 examples of energy transformations. Each group can be given different transformations to prepare the flowcharts. - playing a guitar - light a bulb - light a matchstick - use a bow and arrow - Using an electric kettle - Riding a roller coaster - Using an electric bell - Using a drill to put a nail/screw

Importance of Energy Transformations at home and in the community Did you know that energy transformations have been utilized over the centuries so that people can do work? Remember energy can't be created or destroyed only transformed. Let us watch a video that looks at examples of energy transformations in everyday life over the centuries. Can you identify some new examples of energy transformations from the video? Why are these transformations important. https://youtu.be/9wxbT2g1jtM?si=GsdCumFO1Na7eAQf (5:46 mins) Note: For those teachers without access to technology, please show students pictures of the following energy transformations and get them to explain what is happening: > A person eating a mango as they work in the field (food converted to work)
A person drawing back the string on a bow to fire an arrow (motion of pulling on bow creates forward speed of the arrow-energy transformed form putting tension in the bow)

Wind energy turns the fins on a windmill or water turns the paddles on a water wheel. (The wind or water turns a wheel which can be used as energy for pumping water, grinding grain, lifting objects, creating electricity. Etc.) These are examples of renewable resources because they continue to flow naturally with no additional work on our part and they should last forever unlike oil or natural gas which eventually will be entirely used up- then what do we do for energy?
Sunlight shines on a coconut tree. (the energy from the sun is converted into food in the form of a coconut which can further be used by humans as food in order to do work)
Wood is burned to create heat. (the wood has stored chemicals that are capable of being ignited as a fire and to produce heat as a biproduct of combustion)

Pictures of the industries and businesses can be present to stimulate discussion. - What activities do we use energy for at home and in our community? - What industries/ businesses are present in your community? Where do they get their energy from? - How is it stored so it can be drawn from when needed? - How is the energy used? Is it used in only one form? The teacher will use a graphic organizer to record the information from the discussion.

Example: paint factory, bakery. The students can fill out the graphic organizer while on the field trip/ listening to the professionals speak about energy.

The teacher will guide the discussion using the local businesses and industries for suitable examples.

The negative effects of energy transformations can be added to the graphic organizer. Energy Transformations and advances in Technology The teacher will pose the following question to the students: Can we bend light? Materials: plastic bottle, laser pointer, drill/ice pick, water The teacher can demonstrate fibre optics by: drilling a hole through a plastic bottle about half-way up the bottle. Fill the bottle with water and turn down the lights. Shine a cheap laser pointer light through the side of the bottle directly across the bottle to the opposing hole. As the water leaves the bottle through that open hole, you can see the light internally reflecting down the water stream exactly like a glass fibre. We can bend light energy! The students can be shown the following video that demonstrates the bending of light. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hBQ8fh_Fp04 (1:18 min)

Teacher will ask the questions after students view the video: Light bends when it moves from one medium to another e.g. when it moves from air to water. is the name given to the bending of light. What name is given to the bending of light? (Refraction). Light refraction power fibre optic cables. Optical fiber is a very thin strand of pure glass which acts as a waveguide for light over long distances What are fibre optics important? (Fibre Optics have the ability to use light to transform energy more efficiently.) Some discussion points: Refraction and energy transformations are important concepts that engineers apply in developing technology and advances in science. Light refraction emerges as a captivating phenomenon central to the functionality of fiber optic cables. One of the key advantages of fiber optic cables is their superior energy efficiency compared to traditional copper cables. Fiber optic cables
transmit data using light signals, which require significantly less energy compared to electrical signals used in copper cables. Thus, the transformation of energy is more efficient for use.
Video that explains how fibre optic work and the uses of fibre optics https://youtu.be/o5t6evogJbg?si=ZPnG_8bCExMsvamg (3:58)
What energy is transferred with the use of fibre optics based on the video? (Light energy is utilized and so energy is transferred from light energy to electrical energy or to transmit signals)

The students will be given a passage to read on Solar Energy. The passage reviews the uses and also points out some of the advantages and disadvantages of using Solar Energy. Students will read together.
Passage: https://www.softschools.com/language_arts/reading_comprehension/science/111/solar_power/

Solar energy is changed into electricity in two ways. Solar cells change sunlight into electricity which are grouped into panels used in a variety of different ways. Sometimes these panels are called solar panels which collect, use and distribute the energy from the Sun. This type of collection is also used to power small cells inside batteries or calculators, but mainly used for power single homes or large power plants.
The second way solar power is used to generate electricity is by focusing the Sun's heat to a fluid that produces steam that is then used to turn a generator.

Solar power systems are very beneficial. First, they do not cause pollution or carbon dioxide. And they have very little impact on the environment. Overall as well, solar energy costs less once the equipment is in place. The energy and heat from the Sun is basically free but it costs money to build the right equipment to collect the power of the Sun.

A couple problems with solar power includes the inconsistency of sunlight reaching the Earth's surface, because the amount of sunlight will vary depending on location, time of day, year, and weather conditions. Also, since the Sun does not deliver much energy to any single place at any one time, a large surface is needed to collect the Sun's energy for it to be useful.

Even though the amount of energy reaching the surface of the Earth throughout the world is greater than the total amount of energy used or needed, the current limitations in collecting this energy will need to be overcome.
The students will be engaged in a classroom discussion aimed at identifying some of the advantages and disadvantages of Solar Energy, drawing on some ideas from the passage.
What are some of the advantages of using solar power? (Good source of light and heat, can generate electricity, environmentally friendly - reduction in carbon emissions)

Solar energy is called a renewable resource because unlike oil it doesn't disappear after we use it. Why would we choose renewable forms of energy? (The amount of oil and gas we can access is limited and we will become reliant on other countries for this type of fuel. This could get very expensive)

Those forms of fuel like coal, gas, and oil are commonly referred to as Fossil Fuels and are non-renewable. Solar energy is readily available and can be used when needed without running out. What fuels do you use at home? (charcoal, propane gas, etc.) Why
are these non-renewable? (They have limited supply because they are obtained from fossil fuels)

Solar power costs money to set up but panels last a long time; Do you think over the long-term solar power can be cost-effective? (Over the long term it saves energy/power costs, so your electricity bill would be much lower)

Solar Oven Project
Students will be required to collaborate with their classmates to build an oven. The purpose of this project is for students to understand that the sun is a source of energy.

Materials: marker/pencil, ruler, box cutter/scissors, tape, black construction paper, pizza box, thermometer, foil paper, saran wrap, oven mitt, dish/plate, the object that will be used for heating up (marshmallow, chocolate)

Steps: 1. Start with a clean pizza box, 2. use a maker/pencil and a ruler, draw a square one inch in from the edges on top of the pizza box, 3. Using the scissors/box cutter, cut out there of the four lines to make a flap
4. cover the inside of the clap with aluminum foil and tape it down securely
5. Line the inside edges of the bottom of the pizza box with aluminum foil strips
6. Line the bottom of the bottom of the pizza box with the black paper
7. Cover the opening with the saran wrap and securely tape one piece of it to the top of the bole and one-piece underneath. Ensure its an airtight window.
8. Place the aluminum pan/plate covered with foil inside your solar oven then your item on the plate.

Students can use their oven to make predictions: How long will the food item take to melt? How hot was the sun? What time of day was the hottest? retrieved from https://jenniferfindley.com/solar-oven-project/?epik=dj0yJnU9M1RRSmQzMXBhYTJoc242dThOUmI2X0prRGFRX0E2LUEmcD0wJm49QUtFbW9xRzZqdTQ0cnRRUFpYUjZvdyZ0PUFBQlFBR1dwTjU4

Students can test their oven by: 1) measuring the temperature of a cup of water every 15 minutes for 2 hours using a timer and a thermometer. Students can draw a simple line graph of temperature on the Y axis (the dependent variable) and time on the X axis (the independent variable) for the data in the table. 2) the length of time it takes to melt a tablespoon of butter/ bar of chocolate/wax. Repeat each substance three times and take the average time.

Students can also sketch their completed oven. label the materials used and the purpose for each. Students can also share the difference observed with their solar oven compared to the oven used at home.

Making a Solar Water Heater You will need: five metres of rubber hose pipe (black works best) two corks, cold water, and a funnel. 1. Cork one end of the hosepipe using the funnel to fill the pipe with cold water. Then cork the open end. 2. Measure the temperature of the cold water that you added by putting some water in a cup and using a thermometer. 3. Wind the pipe in a spiral on the ground in a sunny place. Leave the pipe for one hour. 4. Uncork the pipe and feel the water with your hands. Is it hot or cold? Pour some of the water into a cup and measure the temperature. How does it compare to the water you put into the black hose? Note: Heat energy from the sun warms the hose and the water inside.

Some people use such black pipes or (black barrels on the roof) to preheat water for use in their homes. Retrieved from https://housing.com/news/overhead-water-tank-location-and-position-as-per-vastu/ Is black the best color for our hoses for heating water? Students will conduct an experiment. What you need: four clear colourless plastic bottles the same size, white paint, black paint, aluminium foil, water, thermometer, measuring cup - Plastic bottle 1 - paint white - Plastic bottle 2 - paint black - Plastic bottle 3 - cover in foil - Plastic bottle 4 - keep clear and colourless Measure 4 cups of water and place in a large bowl. After 5 minutes measure the temperature of the water. Using the water from the bowl measure 1/2 cup of water or an appropriate volume and place it in each plastic bottle. Place the bottles outside in the sun for two hours. Measure the
temperature of the water in each bottle. Students record their measurements in a table. (Black should be the hottest.)

Without light, there is no color. When people see colors, they are really seeing different types of light bouncing off objects. Light is made up of wavelengths of light, and each wavelength is a particular colour. Objects appear to be different colours because they absorb some colours (wavelengths) and reflect or transmit other colours. The colours we see are the wavelengths that are reflected or transmitted. White objects appear white because they reflect all colours. Black objects absorb all colours so no light is reflected. Thus all the sun’s heat was kept to keep the water the hottest in the plastic bottles.

Light and Color
• Transmission
  • Light passes straight through (no change)
  • Examples
• Absorption
  • Light absorbed (turn it into heat)
• Reflection
  • Light is bounced off an object.
  • Examples

inclusive_learning_strategies: Introducing Natural Resources: Students, do we use things from nature? (Yes) Let us go outside and find some of those things that we use from nature. Ask students to record the resources they identify. Note to teacher: For example: Wind, water, trees, soil, plants, animals, rocks, sunlight. When students return to the classroom, generate a list of ONLY natural resources. Where needed, explain to students why some of the things they listed are not natural resources. Students, all the things that we have identified are called natural resources. Hold a discussion on the natural resources that they have identified and how these are used. Activity: Look around the classroom and list as many items as you can that are made from natural resources. Let’s watch the short video below to learn some more about natural resources: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=axCR3uIn3Vs (0-0:28 mins)

As you watch the video be prepared to answer questions about the following: 1. What is a natural resource? (A natural resource is a part of the earth that people use.) 2. What are some examples of natural resources? (Ans: sunlight, wind, water, plants and animals and fossil fuels like, coal, crude oil and natural gas) 3. What are the new natural resources that you didn’t know before? (coal, crude oil and natural gas) As we would have learnt, natural resources provide us with many different things. Natural resources provide the raw materials necessary for various human activities, including the production of energy. Renewable resources and non-renewable resources: You would have also learnt from the video that natural resources can be divided into two main groups: renewable resources and non-renewable resources. Let us continue watching the short video to find out the differences between renewable and non-renewable resources. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=axCR3uIn3Vs (0:28 - 1:16 mins)
As you watch the video, listen for the following: 1. Differences between renewable and non-renewable resources. 2. Examples of renewable resources and two examples of non-renewable resources.
From the video we see that natural resources can be divided into two main groups: renewable and non-renewable. From the video, let us now write a definition for these terms.

Renewable resources are replaced or replenished by nature when they are used so they won't run out. They can be used now and by future generations too. Sunlight, wind and water are examples of renewable resources.

Non-renewable resources on the other hand are not able to be replenished by nature at the rate at which they are being used whenever. Examples of non-renewable resources are crude oil, coal and natural gas.

Natural Energy resources: As mentioned above, natural resources provide us with the energy we need to cook, run vehicles, factories, light, etc. The natural resources that provide us with energy are called natural energy resources.

Note to Teacher: Discuss the importance of energy in everyday life with the students. Include a talk about common sources of energy that may have been observed on the nature walk that children are familiar with, such as sunlight, wind, and water.

Team Non-renewable Energy Resources: Subgroups: Coal, Natural gas, Crude oil.

Coal pit
