On the cardstock sheet provided, have students create a poster/model showing the natural energy resource they have been assigned. (Research/Teacher provides). Their poster/model should also include a few sentences about the device or the energy resource. Example of a poster Renewable Energy Sources Non-Renewable Energy Solar Wind Hydro Biomass Geothermal Tide Fossil Fuels Coal Nuclear Natural Gas Figure 1. Renewable and non-renewable energy sources.
Have each group present their work to the class, then display the work on the classroom wall. Ensure that all of the students are able to identify the forms of energy, or the devices displayed.
Let students state: 1. Which ones they had not seen or used before. 2. Which ones are renewable energy resources, and which one were non-renewable energy resources
Renewable energy sources Let us now have a closer look at the energy sources grouped as renewable energy, to see how they are formed, extracted and prepared for use.
Fossil fuels Coal, crude oil and natural gas are a group of fuels called fossil fuels. A fuel is any substance/material that when burnt, produces energy.

Fossil fuels were literally formed from the fossils or the remains of plants and animals that lived millions of years ago. It is important that students have an appreciation for the fact that these fuels were formed a long time ago and they take a very long time to be formed.

Natural Gas Characteristics of natural gas Natural gas is comprised of many different compounds. However, the main component is methane gas. In its pure form, it is colourless and odourless. How is natural gas formed The process that creates natural gas, is the same as the process that creates crude oil. Natural gas is generally found, in the same areas that crude oil is found.

COAL: What does coal look like? Black/brown or black sedimentary rock which is composed mainly of carbon and hydrogen. Usually found underground. There are four different types of coal. How is coal formed? Coal was formed when dead plant matter buried in swampy environments is subjected to heat and pressure over hundreds of millions of years. Over time, the plant matter is transformed to coal.

Crude Oil: Characteristics of crude oil: Crude oil is a smelly, yellow-black liquid, usually found underground. It can also be yellowish, reddish, tan, or even greenish. It consists of many different compounds. How is crude oil formed? Millions of years ago, the remains (fossils) of marine animals and plants were covered by layers of sand and silt. Over a very long period of time, heat and pressure from these layers, turned the fossils into what we know as crude oil.

After students have studied the information on the posters and watched the videos, lead students on a discussion centred around the following concepts: 1. How fossil fuels are formed and the time it takes for them to be formed. 2. Reasons why fossil fuels have been placed in the category of non-renewable energy. 3. If it is possible to use all of the fossil fuel that has been mined/drilled from the Earth or we are aware of. 4. What might be the consequences of No. 3 happening.

Extracting fossil fuels and preparing them for use: Now you know how fossil fuels are formed and where they are found. Let us now look at how they are extracted from the Earth and how they are prepared before they are used to provide energy.

Natural gas: Let us look at the following video to find out how natural gas is formed, extracted and processed before use. Natural gas https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-njmj0diWu8 (3:38 mins). Once natural gas is found, it is transported to the surface by a well. Natural gas can be pumped out through the same wells used for extracting crude oil. After it is brought to the surface, it then flows into large pipelines. At a gas processing plant, certain gases found along with the methane gas are separated from the methane and cleaned.
Why do you think it is important to remove the unwanted substances from the coal before it is used?

Coal: Coal buried below the surface of the Earth, can be extracted by underground or surface mining. Retrieved from: https://pixabay.com/photos/industry-dumper-minerals-coal-2023592/ https://pixabay.com/photos/coal-briquette-black-471904/ The mined coal is taken to a plant, where it is cleaned and processed, to remove unwanted substances such as dirt, Sulphur, ash, etc. The coal is then broken into smaller usable lumps for use in domestic settings. Why do you think it is important to remove the unwanted substances from the coal before it is used?
Crude oil: How do we get oil from the ground https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vjcfXqMt5D (1:24 mins)
Where Do We Get Oil From? https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xZ1HIBIIJU0 (1:31 mins)

Before crude oil can be used it has to be converted into various finished products through a process known as refining., which is a series of processes that transform it into useful products like gasoline, diesel and jet fuel. Crude oil distillation is the first process in the refining journey. Crude oil is also used for the manufacturing of plastics. The following simulation helps to explain the processes that take place at a refinery. Animation showing the process of refining crude oil through fractional distillation https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=COm8ute0VJs (0:34 mins)

So, what are the different fossil fuels used for? Coal: solid fossil fuel that is used for heating homes, cooking and generating power plants. Natural gas: Natural gas is widely used for cooking and for heating homes. Crude oil: Many substances are obtained from the refining of crude oil. Refined products obtained from crude oil have several uses. While some are used as fuels, some are used for other purposes. Uses of some of the more well-known fuels are shown in the table below. SUBSTANCE | USES: Butane and propane | For cooking, heating and transportation; Gasoline | Fuel for cars; Diesel | Fuel for cars and heavier vehicles such as lorries and buses; Kerosene | To power aircrafts; Fuel oil | Fuel for ships and power stations

Activities: 1. As a whole class, generate a list of machines that have an engine to 'burn' fossil fuels to generate power, the fuel each uses and one function of the equipment. Encourage kids to think of things that they use. Examples: lawn mower, quad, dirt bikes, etc.
Homework: Ask students to identify 5 different fuel-burning sources at home.

From what we have covered so far, you would have seen that, to extract fossil fuels, we have to bore into the Earth’s crust, in some cases hundreds of metres. These fossil fuels then must be processed and transported over very long distances.

Impact of fossil fuels on the environment: Although fossil fuels have made life easier, finding, extracting, refining, transporting and using fossil fuels, all have negative effects on the environment. Let us continue watching the video to find out how fossil fuels impact the environment: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=axCR3uIn3Vs (1:45 - 400 min)

As you watch the video, pay attention to the different ways that coal, oil and gas impact the environment, keeping the following questions in mind. Questions: 1. What are two ways that the mining of coal or the drilling of oil or natural gas may impact the land? (Landscape changes and habitat loss. Destruction of living things. Chemical use for extraction and effluent pollutes the air and land.) 2. What impact does fossil fuels have on our water bodies? (Pollution of waterways from oil spills. Rising water levels due to global warming.) 3. Air pollution is one of the ways that fossil fuels impact the environment and organisms. State two ways how this can happen. (Burning of fuels gives off gases that pollute the air. The emissions from power plants can combine with water in the air to make acid rain which kills plants and potentially destroys farmlands.)
Discuss with the students the differences between surface and underground mining of coal. Also discuss the effects that mining and drilling may have on the environment.
List two ways we use coal today.
Why do you think coal is still being used today, even though it causes many environmental problems?

Cookie mining activity: Below is an investigation using chocolate chip cookies (similar models using plasticine or clay, can be used instead) as a model for what happens to the environment when fossil fuels are extracted. Today, you will get an opportunity to play the role of a miner in a simulation exercise, but you will not be mining coal, you will be mining chocolate chips from a chocolate chip cookie, using a toothpick.

Chocolate chip cookie - Retrieved from: https://pixabay.com/photos/cookie-chocolate-chip-cookie-snack-1264231/ Before you start the activity, there are a few rules you must follow. You must wash your hands before you receive your cookie. The chocolate chips are not to be eaten before the end of the activity. After you receive your cookie, draw your cookie in the top left-hand box of the table on the worksheet provided. After you have drawn your cookie, you must now draw an imaginary habitat in the top right-hand box of the table. Your habitat must contain animals, plants, a water body, etc.
Now mine the chocolate chips from your cookie using the toothpick supplied.
After you get to a point where it is not possible to mine anymore cookies, draw your cookie or the pieces of cookie remaining, in the bottom left-hand box in your table.

Look at the damage done to your cookie, and imagine similar damage being done to your habitat. How would your habitat be impacted? What would it look like? What parts of your habitat would be affected? Now draw a picture in the bottom right-hand box of your table, to show what your habitat may look like, after it was damaged from the mining.
Was it difficult to mine chocolate chips from the surface or from deep in the cookie?
What could you have done to make mining the chocolate chips easier? (Wetting the cookies).
State two damages that could result from mining coal or extracting oil.
Highlight that the cookie represents the environment, the chocolate chips represent fossil fuels.

Air pollution caused by fossil fuels: Activity: Investigating the Amount of Air Pollution caused by Different Fuels. When fossil fuels are burnt to produce heat or electricity, they release certain gases into the air that can cause air pollution. The following activity, comparing the amount of air pollution caused by two different fuels, should be done by the teacher, as a demonstration: Materials: Spirit lamp with kerosene oil, gas stove, two metal lids or wires, potholder, matches/lighter. white tissue/cloth. Method: Hold the metal lid or wire above the flame spirit lamp flame for 30s to capture the soot that forms. Caution: The lid will become very hot please handle with care. Once the lid/wire has cooled, label it A. Have students observe the lid/wire once it has cooled. Wipe the lid or wire with a white tissue or cloth and make observations. Repeat the experiment by holding the other lid/wire over the flame of a stove for 30s. Label the cooled lid/wire B. Wipe the lid or wire with a white tissue or cloth and make observations. Compare the amount of soot on each tissue/cloth.
Questions for discussion: How did each tissue appear after being wiped? (There was a black solid on one of the tissues)
2. Which lid/wire made the tissue made the tissue dirtier (The lid heated with the kerosene oil flame became black(er) than the lid heated over the stove)
3. What is the black substance? (Soot)

Emission of Greenhouse gases: The emission of greenhouse gases, causing the Earth to heat up, to temperatures, above which is sustainable, is another impact of the use of fossil fuels. This is known as the greenhouse effect. Discuss with students the greenhouse effect, global warming and climate change. With additional materials on these topics, ask students to create a poster, similar to the one shown below.

Students can also be taken to the following sites where they would be able to manipulate several variables to see the greenhouse effect. https://phet.colorado.edu/sims/html/greenhouse-effect/latest/greenhouse-effect_all.html. An accompanying worksheet can be found in the assessment section.

The transportation of crude oil and natural gases: Another problem with fossil fuels is their transportation, often over hundreds of kilometres. Most oil spills are the result of accidents at oil wells or on the pipelines, ships, trains and trucks that move oil from wells to refineries. Oil spills contaminate soil and water and may cause devastating explosions and fires. Oil spills can also damage or kill wildlife and destroy habitats of many organisms.
One of the most devastating oil spills occurred in 1989 in Prince William Sound, Alaska. The video below gives more information on this oil spill.

Another oil spill, which occurred in Trinidad and Tobago, happened, off the coast of Tobago, in February 2024. As you watch these videos, pay attention to the effects of the spills on the environment and organisms living in the areas. Also look to see what was done to clean up the spills in each case.
Exxon Valdez oil spill harmed wildlife: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CVm1pB3jJOw (2:25 mins)
Oil spill in Trinidad & Tobago: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FRApkdzKheM (1:36 mins)

Cleaning up oil spills: When an oil spill occurs, it is very important that as much of the oil as possible is removed from the area where the spill occurred. Attention must also be given to the animals in the area that are affected and to remove those not affected to safer areas if possible. There are several methods currently available to clean up oil spills. These include skimming the oil off the water, using chemicals to help to break up the oil and burning the oil.

Clean-up simulation exercise: Activity: Imagine that you have an oil spill in a dish of water. Design an experiment, to clean-up this oil spill using three different methods. From the videos and from the different activities you have done, you would have gathered that fossil fuels as energy sources, can have very devastating effects on the environment.

Problems with fossil fuels
- Fossil fuels are non-renewable
They are dangerous to produce.
- Can cause land/water pollution from oil spills or chemicals used to extract the fuels.
- Destruction of habitats
- Air pollution and smog formation.
- Emissions of greenhouse gases.

Do you know of any other problems associated with fossil fuels?

Class activity
- Lead a class discussion about the environmental impact made by each type of fuel and energy-producing device from the video. Discuss how these impacts can affect the environment, human beings, plants and other organisms.
- Generate a class list of the types of impacts that the problems listed could have on the environment, person, animals and plants. Write students’ responses on the board.
- As a whole class, generate a list of machines that have an engine to ‘burn’ fossil fuels to generate power. Encourage kids to think of things that they use. Examples: lawn mower, quad, dirt bikes, etc.
- Can you think of three types of transport that do not burn fuel to make them work? For each one given, state where you think the energy comes from to make it go.

Advantages of fossil fuels
Despite the many problems associated with fossil fuels, they have remained the major sources of energy used the world over. This is due to the fact that they have several advantages. A few of them are listed below.
- The energy produced by fossil fuels is greater than that produced by other energy resources.
- Many products sold nowadays were developed thanks to fossil fuels, for example computers.
- Fossil fuels are both cheap and reliable.
- Transport of fossil fuels is very easy as it is usually done through pipes.
- Fossil fuels are easier to extract and process than the renewable forms of energy.

Do you know of any other advantages of fossil fuels?

Working in their groups, students could be asked to state how each advantage is important.

Activity
After students have gone through the advantages and disadvantages in their groups, listed above and have done and have done the exercises, organize a class debate on the following topic
“Do the advantages of fossil fuels outweigh the problems associated with their extraction, processing, transport and use?”

Nuclear energy another form of non-renewable energy
Nuclear energy comes from radioactive elements, mainly uranium, which is extracted from mined ore and then refined into fuel.

[Image of nuclear power plant]

Nuclear Energy
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uvhi4vju1TY (2:45 mins)

As you watch the video, pay close attention to how nuclear energy is formed and its effect on the environment.
- Found in the centre of atoms – tiny particles that make up every object in the universe.
- Energy is released from these atoms by a process called nuclear fission.
- During ‘nuclear fission’, atoms are broken apart and the energy stored in their centre is released.
- This energy is used to produce steam. The steam turns huge turbines, which drive generators that make electricity.
- Nuclear-generated energy releases no carbon dioxide into the atmosphere.
The waste that is produced must be stored carefully so that it does not come into contact with the outside environment, where it can cause serious damage to all organisms.
Why do you think nuclear energy is not produced in all countries of the world?

Renewable energy and Sustainable energy: Fossil fuels are not only harmful to the planet but one day the world will run out of fossil fuels, as we are using them faster than they can be replenished. So, what can we do to save the planet, reduce our dependence on fossil fuels and combat global warming? One key solution is sustainable energy use.

Sustainability refers to the concept that all people can meet their basic needs infinitely, without compromising future generations. Sustainable energy meets our demand for energy without any risk of going bad or running out.
Examples of sustainable energy sources include wind, solar and water (hydropower). To help us gather more information on sustainable energy use, let us watch the following video.

In a previous lesson, you learnt that renewable resources are natural resources that can be replenished at a higher rate than they are consumed. Therefore, using renewable energy resources forms a significant part of sustainable energy use. In this section, you will look closer at these renewable energy resources and the advantages and disadvantages of producing and using these energy resources. You will also get a chance to demonstrate your creativity and innovation, as you create models of different energy resources.
Sustainable Energy for All: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7PrMaAY39sY (0:50 mins)
Teacher, introduce each of the renewable energy resources by showing photographs or videos of each type.
A series of questions related to each type can then be asked.
Sample questions on solar energy are given below. These can be used as a guide for the other renewable energy resources.

Solar Energy: What types of energy do we get from the sun? (light, heat); How do we use it in our homes? (dry clothes, light rooms, dry plant materials); Is solar energy used in your country?; What can we use the transformed energy for?; What kind of work can the device do for us?
Teacher, show a photo of a solar plant/solar panels. Have you seen these before? If so, where?
Discussion should highlight why solar panels are seen in bright sunny spots. Let students share their thoughts.
Teacher, explain how sunlight can be captured using solar panels to generate electricity or heat water.
Suggested resources and suggested technological activity for each renewable energy source are shown below.

Suggested projects: Solar energy - A solar oven or solar cooker; Wind energy - A wind turbine; Hydropower - A model water wheel; Biomass energy - Observing the fermentation of yeast; Tidal/wave energy - Wave turbine model; Geothermal energy - Model geothermal power plant.

Students can also be taken to a place where solar panels have been installed. Have someone point out the main components of the devices and explain how they work. Solar energy can also be demonstrated using a small solar panel to power a toy car.

Note to teacher: You are encouraged to take the students on a field trip to observe the power plants available in your country. A panel of local energy experts can also be invited to visit the classroom to discuss with students’ examples of alternative energy sources being used in their country.

Advantages and disadvantages of renewable sources: While we know that renewable sources of energy can be replaced over a short period of time and that they are good for the environment, some environmental issues have been associated with their production and use. We would now take a closer look at some of these issues.

Advantages and disadvantages of renewable sources table: Solar energy (Advantages: Produces no toxic pollution or global warming emissions; Disadvantages: Habitat loss, Solar energy is expensive). Wind energy (Advantages: Produces no toxic pollution or global warming emissions; Disadvantages: Habitat loss, Affect wildlife such as birds). Hydropower (Advantages: Produces no toxic pollution or global warming emissions; Disadvantages: Can flood land upstream, Dams can alter the natural flow of water in an area). Geothermal energy (Advantages: Produces no toxic pollution or global warming emissions; Disadvantages: Geothermal system can be very costly to install, Limited to areas with volcanoes and geysers, etc.). Biomass energy (Advantages: When burnt, biomass may produce less pollution than fossil fuels; Disadvantages: Use is limited as there are only a few sites to produce enough power). Tidal/wave energy (Advantages: Is able to provide a more predictable source of energy; Disadvantages: Large areas of land are needed to plant the plants needed, In some areas, forest are being destroyed to plant these crops).
Have students discuss these impacts.

Now that you know the advantages and disadvantages of using alternative energy sources, we will now organise a class debate on the following topic: Would you consider using an alternative energy source to power your car or cool your home? Why or why not?
Ask students to design a poster to depict the following: What will happen if renewable alternative energy sources are not developed and used widely by the general public.

Activities: Divide the class into five groups and assign one of the following to each group. Working in your group, prepare an advertisement to sell one of the following. When you are done, you will present your advertisement to the class. 1. A house heated by geothermal energy. 2. A property which has solar panels. 3. A machine that captures and stores solar energy. 4. A house that is powered by wind energy. 5. A house where everything is powered by the Sun.

The impact of energy technologies: Over the last set of lessons, we looked at both renewable and non-renewable sources of energy – how they are formed, extracted, processed and transported. We have also looked at the environmental impact of these fuels. You would therefore have seen that several technologies are involved in the energy industry. These technologies are crucial to our daily lives. Without them, we would not have the ability to power our vehicles, air
conditioners or heaters or have warm water. However, these technologies have both positive and negative effects on the environment.
Technology can also be misused and abused
Abusing technology
Buying equipment/devices that are not needed.
Energy exploration by oil and gas companies that are not warranted.
Wasting energy simply because it can be afforded.
Misusing technology
This is where a technology is used for a purpose it was not intended for.
One of the best examples of the misuse of energy technology is the use of nuclear energy for the development of nuclear weapons.
What are some other examples of the misuse of technology you can think of?
Conservation

We all must play our part in reducing the amount of energy we use, which will help to reduce the negative impact on the environment. Conservation means to use less of something. If nothing else, we need to use the resources we have more efficiently.
Every action counts! What can individuals do to reduce the amount of energy they use? What do you do to save energy? What else could you do?
Working in groups, students can be asked to make a list of the things that they think persons can do to reduce the amount of energy they use. Each group can then present their ideas to the class.
Examples could include:
1. Uses buses and trains instead of individual vehicles, as these hold more persons.
2. Use cars less, walk where possible. Use a bike instead of using a vehicle.
3. Recycle, reuse and reduce garbage generated.
4. Turn off lights and other appliances and devices, when not in use.
5. Use sources of clean energy instead of fossil fuels, such as solar energy.
6. Use energy saving bulbs.
7. Energy smart landscaping.
8. Switching to more efficient appliances.
Add to the list that students generate where necessary. Go through the list with students to find out how they think each suggestion would help to reduce the use of energy.

Ask students to look at the list and state some of the things that they are currently doing to reduce their energy use. Also ask students to list other things that they may be able to do to reduce their energy use.
Invite someone from a well-known business to tell students what they are doing to reduce their energy use.
Activity – School energy audit
Divide the class into groups of three or four students. Have students in each group, conduct an energy audit in the school, during which time, they will identify areas where energy is being wasted.
After conducting the energy audit, they will discuss their findings and then come up with measures that the school could put in place to save energy.
Each group will then write an energy management action plan for the school. Each group will present their plan and the class will discuss the proposal put forward by each group.
Homework assignment
With the help of an adult, conduct an energy audit in your home. Look out for the following:
1. Areas where energy is being wasted.
2. Activities designed to save energy.
3. Any energy saving devices/equipment found in the home.
4. After conducting the energy audit, discuss your findings with members of your family and suggest different measures that can be put in place to save energy in the home.
List some of the things you are doing or could do, to save energy.
Ask the pupils to each share one thing they are going to do at home to reduce their energy consumption.
Students can research and propose renewable energy options for their school, such as solar heating or photovoltaic cells, or a wind turbine.

Culminating activity: The OECS territories use a lot of energy however they are not producers of the fossil fuels needed for generating that energy. For example, the electricity needed to power our homes, factories and industries come from the burning of fossil fuels. This fuel has to be imported from oil producing countries at very high costs. As a result, the energy consumption bills in the region are very high.

Activity: Have learners conduct research to demonstrate that they can obtain and combine information on the following: the energy and fuels used in their territory, the main sources of the energy and fuels in their territory, how the energy and fuels are used in their territory. Students may complete a table such as the one below, some possible answers have been included for teachers to be guided:

The uses of energy can be placed in 4 main categories as shown in the table below. Sector: Home, Energy source: Wood, coal, Uses: Cooking, cooling and heating homes. Sector: Transportation sector, Energy source: Gasoline, diesel, Uses: Transport people, goods and animals by buses, cars, trucks and planes. Sector: Industrial, Energy source: Coal, wood and diesel, Uses: For operating industrial motors and machinery. Producing electricity. Sector: Commercial sector, Energy source: Light, heating, cooking, security, Uses: Coal, charcoal, LPG, natural gas, nuclear energy.

From the list of energy resources used in your country, choose one then prepare a presentation on that energy resource. Your presentation must include the following: 1. What is the natural source it is derived from? 2. What is this energy resource used for? 3. How it addresses energy needs in your country. 4. The positive and negative effects that this energy resource has on the environment. 5. The role of technology in improving or mediating the environmental effects of using the given energy resource.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Construct an argument that plants and animals have internal and external structures that function to support survival, growth, behavior, and reproduction

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Define the terms: survive, seed dispersal, ejected, evaporation, transpiration
Demonstrate with examples, they understand that animals and plants have special features that allow them to survive.
Recall the names of organs in the human body and state their inherent function for survival.
Explain how some features of organisms enable their survival in their habitats. (ST 2 LS ECS 5)
Explain with examples what are endangered species. (ST 2 LS ECS 12)
Draw and label the main eternal parts of a plant. (ST 2 LS SF 1)
Define the term dispersal and list the agents of seed dispersal. (ST 6 LS DC 10)
Describe the physical features of the parts of a plant, relating these features to their functions. (ST 3 LS SF 1)
Describe the eternal features of animals relating these structures to their functions in the animals. (ST 3 LS SF 2)
Identify that the transport system is a major system in plants and describe the function of its parts. (ST 5 LS SF 3)
Observe pictures & videos that allow you to make inferences about the link between features and function,
Infer what advantage these internal or external structures have in promoting survival.
Tabulate and classify the animal features and their impact.
Research instances of body parts having specific functions for survival.
Communicate understanding by creating a poster.
When conducting practical and group work, display sensitivity and offer assistance to peers who may have physical or learning challenges.
Participate actively in classroom discussions.
Investigate the physical, emotional and social adjustments that occur with organ donation.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Animal Parts and Functions to Survive: Students will research each of the following animals and their particular feature that allows them to survive. They should enter their research results in the table below.

Courting Features and Behaviors: For animals to produce their own offspring, a male and female of the species will often form a parenting couple. This often means the male will try to attract the female with colorful displays and sometimes even dances!

Have students research a Caribbean animal that uses a courting display (using external body features) in order to attract a female thereby ensuring reproduction of offspring and survival of the species. Students should create a poster with pictures of the display and a description of the animal species.
Rubric: Poster pictures (5 marks), Description of species habitat (3 marks), Description of survival function (2 marks), Grammar and punctuation (2 marks)

Plant Survival: How do you think the plant structure in this picture helps this tree produce offspring and survive? See: https://mynaturenook.com/helicopter-seeds/ (wind carries the seeds like a helicopter)
Importance of Water to Plants: Have students sketch a picture of a plant, label the parts and use the picture to explain the process of transpiration.

Internal Organs Help Us Survive: Donation?: Teacher should introduce topic of organ donation and elicit opinions from students on the ethics and viability of the practice. (see information below) - Many countries have programs that allow families of perished accident victims to donate organs to those that have diseased organs but are otherwise healthy. - Also, because we have two kidneys it is very common for a family member to donate a kidney to another family member whose kidneys no longer function.

This can allow the person with kidney problems to live a normal lifestyle. In all organ transplants the donor and recipients must have very similar blood characteristics in order for the new organ to work properly in the recipient body.
Have learners recount a story of friend or family member who may have had an organ transplant. Ask them to identify the lifestyle change before and after the transplant.

Endangered Species: Some animal parts like the tusks of elephants have important functions Digging holes, moving branches, accessing food) (d. They are however valued by humans for other reasons like making jewellery or house decorations etc.)

Write a one-page essay about an endangered species in eth Caribbean whose body partis highly valued for reasons other than what was intended for the plant or animal. Rubric: Description of the Problem (5 marks), Grammar & punctuation (5 marks), Pictures if included (3 marks), How to solve the issue (2 marks)

Other Examples of Bodily Function: Students may do research on the uniqueness of certain species in their respective countries. - Students may research the St Lucia whiptail lizard (Cnemidophorus vanzoi) or other local species of the lizard found in their island. - The armoured body of crabs and the use of their claws for digging. - Snakes make use of the fangs - Scorpions make use of their stinger and claws. - Turtles use their back flippers to dig a nest in the sand.
inclusive_learning_strategies: Looking for Prior Knowledge on Survival Features in Humans: Students, I want you to think about the way our bodies are designed to help us survive, especially in the early days when humans didn't have all the machines to help us with our work or stores to buy our food!
What do our teeth help us do? (cut food, chew food, grind food for eating)
What do our long strong arms help us to do? (reach for food like apples in high branches, swim across waterways and lift materials to build shelter)
What do our strong legs and upright stature help us to do? (chase/hunt animals for food, escape from predators)
What does our nose help us do? (smell food to see if it is spoiled/safe to eat, smell animals that may be warning us)
What do our ears help us do? (hear danger in our vicinity like predatory animals, impending storms,)
What does our sense of taste help us do? (taste food to see if it is spoiled/safe to eat, avoid bitter or foods that upset our digestion)
What do our special hands help us do? (with opposing thumbs our hands allow us to grasp things so we can build shelters and use tools and do many daily requirements for surviving)
What about Internal Structures of Humans? Review with students the function of internal organs. How do they work together to help us survive as a living thing?
Heart (pumps blood throughout the body via the circulatory system, supplying oxygen and nutrients to the tissues and removing carbon dioxide and other wastes)
Kidneys (maintaining overall fluid balance, regulating and filtering minerals from blood, filtering waste materials from food, medications, and toxic substances)
Liver (filters blood from intestines and removes harmful chemicals)
Lungs (oxygenates blood to feed muscles; In respiration, oxygen from incoming air enters the blood, and carbon dioxide, a waste gas from the metabolism, leaves the blood).

Stomach (intestines) have 3 functions: - temporary storage for food, which passes from the esophagus to the stomach where it is held for 2 hours or longer. - mixing and breakdown of food by contraction and relaxation of the muscle layers in the stomach. - digestion of food
So you see students, we have many bodily features that help us to stay healthy and continue living.
Read Aloud Books to Support Function of Animal Parts Sandra Markle Authored Books What if You Had Animal Teeth? https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_vLAx4veHhc (9:15 mins)
What if You Had Animal Eyes? https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_vLAx4veHhc (13:16 mins)
What if You Had Animal Ears? https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=tWV0SEnI7gc (9:33 mins)

Plant Features for Survival: Did you know that plants also have parts and that have a function to help them grow and protect them so they can survive? Let us look at some examples: How do the thorns on a rose bush function to help it survive? (animals that get too close to the plant that might destroy its flowers will get pricked and decide to stay away) Some plants (poison oak, poison ivy, stinging nettle) have oils in their leaves that are very irritating to animals. How would that help the plant survive? (animals would not want to brush up against the plant to hurts its leaves or eat it for food as it would cause the animal great discomfort) Some plants have small hairlike structures that sense an animal like a housefly on them and react. The Venus flytrap actually captures the fly and digests the fly for food- a form of survival.
Venus flytrap picture retrieved from: https://www.nature.org/en-us/about-us/where-we-work/united-states/north-carolina/stories-in-north-carolina/pitcher-venus-flytrap-carnivorous-plants/
See the flytrap in action here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=O7eQKSf0LmY (2.50 mins)
Other plants will close or move if they are touched. (e.g. mimosa pudica) to protect against being eaten or to avoid losing water through their leaves (dehydration)
See the mimosa pudica moving here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xEQJ0byHMXw (6:14 mins)

Plants can also ensure their survival by making sure their seeds get to fertile ground. This is called seed dispersal and it happens by many processes. Sometimes their seeds are ejected from a pod, carried by the wind or by water or by animals (on coats or in stomach) to other locations where they flourish. They have special features likes wings, waterproof seed coverings or sticky burrs that allow them to move to better locations for surviving.
Examples: Tetraberlinia moreliana (Leguminosae — Caesalpinioideae) Pea Family (Leguminosae)

Transpiration: Plants Need Water! Sun shines of the leaves of plants and warms them up. The liquid water in those leaves gest additional energy and begins to jump into the air as a gas in a process we have already studies called evaporation. The departure of the water from the leaves has the impact of drawing more water up from the soil through the stem. This process is called transpiration and plants could not survive without it. The process works properly because of the specialized parts in the plant.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Use a model to describe that animals receive different information through their senses, process it in their brain and respond to it differently

specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: Learners are expected to: Knowledge
Define the terms: senses, nervous system, nerves, spinal cord, braille
Identify the five senses.
Account for the importance of each of the senses in human survival.
Give at least three examples of how animals use their specialized senses.
Draw a diagram to show they understand the input of sensory information to the brain and an output of bodily response.
Infer from a scenario, the steps involved in going from
a sensory input to a bodily response.
Classify sensory inputs.
Classify brain outputs.
Calculate % of global spinal injuries.
Communicate your research of animal use of senses in the form of a poster.
Construct a simple robot. (extended based on resources) for the purposes of mimicking sensory input and brain output.
Record data using bar graphs. (ST 2 ESS ER 2)
Outline what stimulates each of the five senses. (ST 2 LS ECS 14)
Name the organ that is stimulated by sound. (ST 2 LS ECS 15)
Attitudes/Values: Appreciation of the many senses animals access to function in the world safely and survive.
Attitudes/Values: Interest/Curiosity in animal’s unique senses.
Attitudes/Values: Safety and care to avoid spinal cord injuries.
Attitudes/Values: When conducting practical and group work, display sensitivity and offer assistance to peers who may have physical or learning challenges.
Attitudes/Values: Participate actively in classroom discussions.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: Which Senses are Used?
Information Processing in Animals - Matching Write the letter(s) of the sense that matches each of the animal behaviors on the left.

The Sense of Touch: Have students explain in a paragraph or a diagram, the steps of sensory processing for each of these human activities that involve touch. A blind person reads a book written in braille. A physician feels an arm to see if it has a break. A violinist places their fingers on the fingerboard of their violin to play a song. A baker kneads the dough as they prepare a loaf of bread.

What is so special? A Research Project: Students should choose three of the following animals and prepare a poster and explanation why the animal’s sensory systems are especially useful for their survival. 1) What special sensory ability does a housefly possess that makes it so difficult to capture them? (peripheral vision) 2) What special sensory ability does a female mosquito have that allows them to find their next blood meal? (detecting carbon dioxide-breathing animals) 3) What special sensory ability does a colossal squid (Mesonychoteuthis hamiltoni) have that helps with hunting in the ocean? (large eyes to receive minimal light in deep water) 4) What sensory property does the Caribbean white-lipped frog take advantage of in attracting a mate? (the frog sings but also creates a vibration in the earth to accentuate the impact of attracting a female) 5) What sensory property does the Blue-footed Boobie take advantage of in attracting a mate? (the sight of the bright blue feet elicits a response in the female bird) 6) What sensory property does a pod of whales access when they migrate as a group to other waters? (water temperature is sensed and whales will move for better feeding or giving birth to offspring)

Spinal Cord Injuries: The spinal cord is a crucial component of the nervous system that allows animals to transmit sensory information to and from the brain. It is very important to protect against injury to the spinal cord because the results can be very dramatic. If our brains can’t receive information about our sense of touch we have difficulty operating our arms and legs which can seriously affect our mobility. Dangerous activities that can lead to spinal injury include but are not limited to: Jumping from high locations, Diving into unknown bodies of water, Driving in vehicles without seatbelts, Riding bicycles in dangerous terrain without a helmet. Statistics: (2019) retrieved from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9554757/ Globally 900,000 cases of spinal injury

Have the students calculate the % of each region of the total global incidence of spinal injury. From this data they should create a bar graph of country vs. % of spinal injuries globally. e.g. ((incidence/900000) x 100)

Response Paper: Students should write a one-page essay about how their school can be made accessible to students who may be confined to a wheelchair because of spinal cord injury or other debilitating injury/disease.
Engineering Project: Students may decide to build a wheelchair ramp for the school which would access their mathematics and construction skills.

Extension Activity- Robotics: Most rudimentary robots accessible to schools have built in sensors that can be programmed around visual cues to stop, turn, reverse etc. These systems mimic processing undertaken by animal’s brains based on sensory input.

School Projects
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iCc4Di3ycNc (2:46 mins)
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dDQThRpDe1A (3:23 mins)
inclusive_learning_strategies: Review of Senses and Sense Organs Students let’s review our senses – sight, smell, hearing, taste and touch.
Which body organ is used for each sense?
Why do we need these senses? (interact safely in the world, satisfy basic needs of food, warmth, shelter)
Now let’s play a game to see which sense we use for each activity.
Animals Use Their Senses Too! Just like humans, animals gather information from their environment through different sensory organs.

Have students look at the following pictures and suggest how the animals use their senses to receive information and respond. How do you think owls find their prey at night? (Owls use their perfect night vision and hearing to find prey.) Retrieved from: https://hawkwatch.org/raptor-id/raptor-id-fact-sheets/great-horned-owl/

Why do you think dogs make good police assistants? (Dogs have a powerful nose and an amazing sense of smell. Police and military service dogs are trained to detect drugs and explosives.) Retrieved from: https://www.customs.go.jp/moji/english/canine/canine.html
How do you think snakes and lizards find prey? (Snakes & lizards use their tongue for sensing prey) Retrieved from: https://www.petmd.com/reptile/pet_lover/evr_rp_why_snakes_use_tongue

How do you think bats are able to fly in the dark of night? (Bats use echolocation to navigate and find prey. They send out waves of sound from their mouths or noses, which bounce off their surroundings right back to their ears. By listening to the echoes, bats can build up a picture of exactly what’s around them.) Retrieved from: https://www.britannica.com/list/5-surprising-facts-about-bats
How do you think mice avoid cats? (Animals have a sense of smell to sense dangerous predators) Retrieved from: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/House_mouse
Why do you think animals have whiskers? (Animals brush, or whisk, their whiskers against objects to guide them, measure things, and find food). Retrieved from: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Whiskers

How Do Animals Respond to Information from the Senses? The nervous system in animals is made up of sensory organs that collect information and use biochemicals in our bodies and specialized signaling anatomy to pass messages to the brain. The brain interprets the information then sends a message back through the nerves to tell the animal how to respond. (e.g. we touch something hot with our hand and the brain tells us to pull our hand away)

Retrieved from: https://childsuccesscenter.com/home/resources/sensory-processing/
In many animals the sensory information is collected and transferred using something called a nerve receptor. The information can then be passed along a spinal cord to the brain.
Retrieved from: https://www.youtube.com/watch?app=desktop&v=12Cez7IFDY8 (11:52 mins)

Retrieved from: https://www.twinkl.nl/teaching-wiki/nervous-system
What Is the Response from the Brain?
Discuss with students what happens when you give a stray dog some food.
Have students explain the steps in that scenario?
Dog sees food > brain signals it resembles food > dog moves closer
Dog smells food > brain signals it isn’t spoiled > dog begins to eat
Dog tastes food > brain signals it doesn’t taste spoiled > dog continues eating

Retrieved from: https://milaap.org/fundraisers/support-feeding-street-dogs
Another example for students to map out the stepwise response. Scenario: A cat spies a large dog coming towards them from afar and then as it gets closer, hear sit growling
Retrieved from: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cat%E2%80%93dog_relationship
Possible student answers:

resources.description: Solar Oven: Materials: Cardboard box, aluminium foil, plastic wrap, black construction paper, glue, scissors. Instructions: Students can construct a simple solar oven using a cardboard box lined with aluminium foil to reflect sunlight onto a black construction paper-covered surface inside. They can experiment with different designs and angles to optimize heat absorption and cooking efficiency.

Wind Turbine: Materials: Cardboard, wooden dowels or sticks, paper cups, hot glue gun, scissors, string, small DC motor (optional). Instructions: Students can build a small-scale wind turbine model using cardboard blades attached to a central shaft made of wooden dowels. They can mount the turbine on a base and experiment with different blade designs and wind speeds to generate rotational motion.

Hydroelectric Generator: Materials: Plastic bottle, small water pump or motor, plastic tubing, wooden dowels, cardboard, glue, scissors. Instructions: Students can create a hydroelectric generator model by constructing a miniature dam using a plastic bottle filled with water. They can attach a small water pump or motor to the bottle's opening and connect it to a turbine made of cardboard blades. As water flows from the bottle through the turbine, it can generate electricity.

Question on light waves 1. Light is all around us, without light we would not be able to see the things around us. Answer the following questions. a. Light travels in ___________ ___________ b. Light is an example of a ___________ wave.

inclusive_assessment_strategies: General questions on waves motion and wave characteristics
1. Draw a picture of a wave and label it with the following terms. a. Wavelength b. Amplitude c. Crest d. Trough

2. You encounter many different waves in your daily life. a. Give two examples of waves that you encounter in everyday life. (ocean, skipping rope) b. Give two characteristics that you expect the waves you listed in 2a. to have. (have an amplitude, have a direction) c. Explain how you know that waves have repeating patterns. (same crest and trough repeated) d. Give two reasons why studying waves is important. (waves are used for communication and medical devices)

3. A wave can be considered as a disturbance, moving energy from place to place. For each of the following examples given, state the source of energy for the wave. Wave example: a. Waves moving along a rope. b. Water waves formed in a pool. c. Sound waves formed from the beating of a drum. Rope: moving hand provides energy Waves in a pool: a dropped object provides energy Drum: hand motion beating drum provides energy

4. Below is a picture of a swimming pool. If a stone is thrown into the pool, water waves would form. a. Describe how the water waves would move in relation to the point where the stone was thrown. (in a circle moving from the centre)
Where does the energy to form the wave come from? (the impact of the stone falling – potential & kinetic energy)

Suppose a beach ball was on the surface of the when the stone was thrown into the pool. How would you expect the ball to move after the stone was thrown into the pool? (the ball would ride the crests of the waves and move across the pool)
What would you expect to see if a larger stone was thrown into the pool of water? (a larger wave would be formed because the falling stone would have more energy due to higher mass)
Questions on types of waves

NOTE: Teacher should limit the assessment to simple examples of transverse (rope) longitudinal (slinky) and electromagnetic (light) waves. Some questions that follow can be differentiated to be age and ability appropriate.
1. Transverse waves and Longitudinal waves are two different types of waves. Answer the following questions about these two types of waves.
Define Transverse waves and give two examples of a transverse wave. (skipping rope, ocean wave)
State one way in which a transverse wave is different from a longitudinal wave. (longitudinal wave moves because of compression like a slinky)
2. Waves can also be classified as mechanical waves and electromagnetic waves.
What are mechanical waves? (waves created by a physical movement)
Give one example of a mechanical wave. (stone in a pool)
What are electromagnetic waves? (waves that are created as a result of vibrations between an electric field and a magnetic field)
Give one example of an electromagnetic wave. (different types of light)
3. Slinkys are very useful for modelling longitudinal and transverse waves.
Explain how you would move a Slinky to produce transverse waves. (compress and release a slinky)
Explain how a longitudinal wave moves along a Slinky. (the compressed wire pushes each adjacent wire as the compression is released)
What happens to a transverse wave when it gets to the end of a Slinky? (the energy gets absorbed by hand holding it or it pushes air)

a. What type of wave is this? (longitudinal)
b. Describe how this wave would move along a Slinky.
c. Describe how the slinky would move.
d. What are the areas labelled A and B called?
A__(rarefaction)
B__(compression)

a. What type of waves are sound waves? (longitudinal)
b. What are sounds made by? (vibrations)

2. Pitch and loudness are two terms which can be used to describe sound.
